does: This paper proposes SparseRM, a lightweight reward modeling approach that uses Sparse Autoencoders (SAE) to extract preference-relevant features from LLM representations. The method decomposes LLM hidden states into interpretable sparse directions that capture preference signals, then projects representations onto these directions to compute alignment scores. A simple aggregation head combines these scores to produce final preference predictions. The approach is evaluated on three preference modeling benchmarks where it demonstrates competitive or superior performance compared to mainstream reward models while using less than 1% of the parameters typically required, achieving resource efficiency without substantial performance degradation.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature addressing efficient reward model training for LLM alignment. It connects to prior research on sparse interpretability methods, particularly work applying Sparse Autoencoders to understand LLM representations and extract meaningful features. The paper responds to the challenge of training reliable reward models under resource constraints, extending earlier approaches that use parameter-efficient fine-tuning or knowledge distillation. It also relates to ongoing efforts to improve interpretability in preference learning systems, combining ideas from mechanistic interpretability with practical reward modeling constraints that have been central concerns in post-training alignment research.
enables: SparseRM opens directions for more interpretable and resource-efficient alignment of large models in resource-constrained settings. The use of SAE-extracted features potentially enables better understanding of which model capabilities relate to preference alignment, supporting future work in mechanistic interpretability of reward signals. The lightweight nature suggests applications in scenarios with limited compute budgets, edge deployment, or rapid iteration on alignment. Follow-up work could explore how sparse feature dictionaries generalize across model families and sizes, whether these interpretable directions transfer between models, and how SAE-based approaches might extend to other alignment objectives beyond preference modeling or multi-objective reward design.
